practices: The AP Statistics course framework is structured around four key skills. Skill 1, Selecting Statistical Methods (15–23% of MCQs), involves choosing appropriate methods for collecting and analyzing data. Skill 2, Data Analysis (15–23% of MCQs), focuses on describing patterns, trends, and relationships in data. Skill 3, Using Probability and Simulation (30–40% of MCQs), involves exploring random phenomena through probability calculations and simulations. Skill 4, Statistical Argumentation (25–35% of MCQs), requires developing explanations and justifying conclusions using evidence from data, definitions, and statistical inference.

keyTopics: Representing a Categorical Variable with Tables and Graphs
Representing a Quantitative Variable with Graphs
Describing the Distribution of a Quantitative Variable (Shape, Center, Variability, Unusual Features)
Summary Statistics for a Quantitative Variable (Mean, Median, Quartiles, Standard Deviation)
Graphical Representations of Summary Statistics (Boxplots)
Comparing Distributions of a Quantitative Variable
The Normal Distribution and Z-scores
Representing Two Categorical Variables (Two-way tables, Bar graphs)
Statistics for Two Categorical Variables (Conditional relative frequency)
Representing the Relationship Between Two Quantitative Variables (Scatterplots)
Correlation
Linear Regression Models
Residuals and Residual Plots
Least Squares Regression
Analyzing Departures from Linearity
Introduction to Planning a Study (Observational Studies vs. Experiments)
Random Sampling and Data Collection (Simple Random, Stratified, Cluster, Systematic)
Potential Problems with Sampling (Bias: Voluntary Response, Undercoverage, Nonresponse, Response)
Introduction to Experimental Design (Experimental units, treatments, confounding variables)
Selecting an Experimental Design (Completely Randomized, Randomized Block, Matched Pairs)
Inference and Experiments

examFormat.freeResponse: 6 questions (5 short-answer and 1 investigative task), 90 minutes, 50% of total score

antiPatterns: The CED explicitly de-emphasizes memorization of specific complex formulas. For example, formulas for confidence intervals and test statistics are not provided on the exam formula sheet and do not need to be memorized, as they can be constructed from the general test statistic formula and relevant standard error formulas that are provided. Students are expected to interpret standard computer output rather than perform extensive computations by hand. When drawing conclusions from significance tests, students should not state that they 'accept' or have 'proven' the null hypothesis; instead, they should state that they either 'reject' or 'fail to reject' the null hypothesis based on the evidence.
